lessons.title: Making a micro:bit counter
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a variable holds one value that can change
I can use the micro:bit buttons (inputs) to change a variable
I can plan an algorithm for a counter
I can make and test a counter in MakeCode
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: micro:bit · MakeCode · variable · input · count. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the activity worksheet ready; MakeCode loaded; micro:bits/USB leads out. Read the four 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is a micro:bit? A micro:bit is a tiny computer programmed in MakeCode; its buttons are inputs. Pupils choose what a micro:bit is. Likely error: 'a micro:bit is a phone' → fix-words 'a tiny computer we program.'
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — a variable holds ONE value. Use a real box + number cards: swap one card for another, the box still holds one value. Our variable is 'count'. Support choose what a variable is; Challenge: what happens to the old value (replaced).
3. (18–28) Inputs. Button A, B and A+B are inputs; the battery is power, not an input. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the inputs we use to count.
4. (28–40) Plan then code. Pupils ORDER the algorithm steps, then open MakeCode → New Project → Variables → Make a Variable → count. Model 'Make a Variable'. Then pupils ORDER the counter blocks (Parsons). Support choose which block adds 1 / which resets; Core explain 'set count to 0'.
5. (40–52) You-do — make and test the counter; test with the play button (emulator). Pupils screenshot their work + paste the MakeCode link. Challenge: add an A+B reset. Likely error: forgetting 'set count to 0' → fix-words 'start at zero.' TA: prompt, do not drag blocks for them.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what 'change count by 1' does (adds 1).
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Making a micro:bit timer
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a countdown timer does and how a loop repeats steps
I can find and fix a bug in a program
I can use true/false (Boolean) and if…else to start and stop the timer
I can make and test a countdown timer in MakeCode
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: timer · loop · bug · Boolean · true · false · if…else. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the activity worksheet ready; MakeCode loaded; stopwatches out. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we made a counter.
1. (0–8) Starter — why a timer? A counter alone is not fair (people take different times); a timer gives everyone the same time. A forever loop repeats steps. Pupils recap 'change count by 1' and choose why we add a timer. Likely error: '30 goals is the score' → fix-words '30 goals in HOW LONG?'
2. (8–22) Debug with PRIMM (Predict, Run, Investigate). Show the buggy countdown: it starts at 30 but the bar graph only goes up to 10 — the numbers do not match. Support choose where the bug is; Core write the fix (graph up to 30). Show the fixed code.
3. (22–34) True/false — Boolean. A Boolean is only ever true or false (light-switch analogy). Timer running = true → ON (button A); = false → OFF (button B). Pupils choose what 'true' means; Challenge: why true/false is clearer than 1/0.
4. (34–44) if…else — selection. If Countdown = 0 → show the finish icon; else if Timer running = true → take 1 away, wait, show the graph. Pupils ORDER the timer blocks (Parsons). TA: prompt 'what happens first when it reaches 0?'
5. (44–52) You-do — make and test the timer (button A start, button B stop). Pupils screenshot their work + paste the MakeCode link. Likely error: changing the start number but not the graph 'up to' number → fix-words 'change BOTH numbers.'
6. (52–55) Plenary / end of unit — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what a Boolean can be (true or false). Recap the unit: counter → timer.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
